1: A type: "Remember, O LORD, in David's favor, all the hardships he endured." David's hardships in seeking a dwelling for God are a type of Christ's mission to establish the true temple. John 2:19-21 — "destroy this temple, and in three days I will raise it up" (spoken of his body). David sought a house for God's ark; in Christ, God himself becomes the dwelling among his people permanently (Revelation 21:3,22).
2: A type: "how he swore to the LORD and vowed to the Mighty One of Jacob." David's sworn oath to find a place for the LORD reflects the covenant commitment that Christ fulfills. Hebrews 6:17-18 — "God desired to show more convincingly to the heirs of the promise the unchangeable character of his purpose... so that we who have fled for refuge might have strong encouragement to hold fast to the hope set before us." God's own oath is the guarantee of the messianic promise.
3: A type: "I will not enter my house or get into my bed, I will not give sleep to my eyes or slumber to my eyelids." David's restless urgency for God's dwelling is a type of Christ's zeal. John 2:17 — "Zeal for your house will consume me" (applied to Christ at the temple cleansing). The urgency David felt about establishing a place for God's presence is the urgency Christ embodied in establishing the new meeting place between God and humanity.
4: A type: "until I find a place for the LORD, a dwelling place for the Mighty One of Jacob." The place David sought is not the stone temple but the temple Christ is. Hebrews 9:11-12 — Christ entered "the greater and more perfect tabernacle, not made with hands, that is, not of this creation." The ark found its resting place; the glory of God found its dwelling place in the incarnate Son (John 1:14 — "the Word... dwelt among us").
5: A type: "a dwelling place for the Mighty One of Jacob." The Mighty One who is Jacob's God — the God of covenant — finds his ultimate dwelling in Christ and in the community gathered around him. 1 Corinthians 3:16 — "you are God's temple and God's Spirit dwells in you." Ephesians 2:21-22 — the whole structure grows into "a holy temple in the Lord... a dwelling place for God by the Spirit."
6: A type: "We heard of it in Ephrathah; we found it in the fields of Jaar." The ark's journey from obscurity to the city of David prefigures the hidden preparation of God's purposes in Christ — born obscurely in Bethlehem (= Ephrathah, Micah 5:2; Matthew 2:6), kept hidden until his time (Luke 2:52). The ark found and brought to Jerusalem is the type of the glory of God recognized and enthroned in Christ.
7: A type: "Let us go to his dwelling place; let us worship at his footstool!" The pilgrimage call echoes through the Psalter. Hebrews 12:22 — believers have "come to Mount Zion and to the city of the living God, the heavenly Jerusalem." The footstool at which worship is offered is now the throne of the ascended Christ — Rev 4-5 (the worship of heaven centered on the Lamb).
8: A type: "Arise, O LORD, and go to your resting place, you and the ark of your might." The ark of God's might points to Christ, who is "the power of God and the wisdom of God" (1 Corinthians 1:24). The "resting place" for God is fulfilled in Christ's resurrection and session at the right hand of the Father — Hebrews 1:3 ("sat down at the right hand of the Majesty on high").
9: A type: "Let your priests be clothed with righteousness, and let your saints shout for joy." The priests clothed in righteousness are the believers clothed in Christ's righteousness (2 Corinthians 5:21; Revelation 19:8 — "the righteous deeds of the saints"). Revelation 7:9 — the great multitude before the throne clothed in white robes shouting for joy. The priestly garment of righteousness is the robe Christ provides.
10: A shadow: "For the sake of your servant David, do not turn away the face of your anointed one." The māšîaḥ — the anointed one — whose face must not be turned away. Christ now intercedes from the Father's right hand: Hebrews 7:25 — "he always lives to make intercession for them." The prayer that God not reject the anointed is answered permanently in Christ's accepted intercession before the Father (Hebrews 9:24 — "now to appear in the presence of God for us").
11: A direct: "The LORD swore to David a sure oath from which he will not turn back: 'One of the sons of your body I will set on your throne.'" Acts 2:30 — "David was a prophet, and knowing that God had sworn with an oath to him that he would set one of his descendants on his throne, he spoke about the resurrection of the Christ." Peter directly applies this verse to Christ's resurrection. One of the most explicitly messianic verses in the entire Psalter.
12: A shadow: "If your sons keep my covenant and my testimonies that I shall teach them, their sons also forever shall sit on your throne." The conditional "if" of David's natural sons is resolved by Christ, the one son who kept the covenant perfectly (Hebrews 4:15 — "without sin"). The eternal throne depends on perfect obedience — only Christ provided it. Hebrews 1:8 — "Your throne, O God, is forever and ever, the scepter of uprightness is the scepter of your kingdom."
13: A shadow: "For the LORD has chosen Zion; he has desired it for his dwelling place." The final resting place of God is not the stone temple but the heavenly Jerusalem where God and Christ dwell with their people forever. Revelation 21:3,22 — "Behold, the dwelling place of God is with man... its temple is the Lord God the Almighty and the Lamb." Hebrews 12:22 — the city that believers have come to is the dwelling place God has chosen.
14: A shadow: "This is my resting place forever; here I will dwell, for I have desired it." The permanent, final dwelling of God realized in Christ and the new creation. Revelation 21:3 — "He will dwell with them, and they will be his people, and God himself will be with them as their God." What the ark in Jerusalem temporarily signified, the presence of Christ permanently establishes.
15: A type: "I will abundantly bless her provisions; I will satisfy her poor with bread." Christ satisfies the hungry with bread. John 6:35 — "I am the bread of life; whoever comes to me shall not hunger." The feeding of the five thousand (John 6:1-15) and four thousand (Matthew 15:32-39) demonstrate the abundance of Zion's messianic provisions. Luke 1:53 — "he has filled the hungry with good things."
16: A type: "Her priests I will clothe with salvation, and her saints will shout for joy." Salvation as clothing — Isaiah 61:10 ("he has clothed me with the garments of salvation"), applied to Christ in Luke 4:18 ("he has sent me to proclaim liberty to the captives"). Revelation 7:9 — the great multitude clothed in white robes shouting before the throne. The priestly garment is the salvation Christ provides, worn by all who are his.
17: A direct: "There I will make a horn grow for David; I have prepared a lamp for my anointed." Luke 1:69 — "he has raised up a horn of salvation for us in the house of his servant David." Luke 1:78-79 — "the sunrise from on high will visit us, to give light to those who sit in darkness." This verse is directly quoted and applied to Christ in Zechariah's Benedictus. The horn of strength and the lamp of lineage and light — both fulfilled in Jesus of Nazareth, son of David.
18: A shadow: "His enemies I will clothe with shame, but on him his crown will gleam." The victorious, crowned king after his enemies are defeated. Hebrews 2:9 — "we see him who for a little while was made lower than the angels, namely Jesus, crowned with glory and honor because of the suffering of death." Philippians 2:9-11 — "God has highly exalted him and bestowed on him the name that is above every name." The crown that gleams is the crown of the exalted Christ after the cross.
